$comment: Ground-truth registry for the v0.2 cite-rate pilot. Each entry is a Py3K-era decision (an Accepted/Final PEP) paired with its load-bearing assumption(s) and the historical outcome that refuted or validated each. The cite-rate pilot asks: when the council critiques the decision PEP, does it cite the assumption that history later refuted? ≥70% = pass.

load_bearing_assumptions.text: The Python community will migrate to Python 3 within a 2–3 year window after release.
load_bearing_assumptions.outcome_evidence: Migration tail extended >8 years; Python 2.7 EOL was extended from 2015 to 2020 due to ecosystem inertia. PEP 466 (security backport policy) and the Py2.7 LTS extensions are direct evidence of the timeline assumption breaking.

load_bearing_assumptions.text: Cleaning up Python's design warts justifies a one-time backward-incompatibility cost.
load_bearing_assumptions.outcome_evidence: Most cleanups (str/bytes split, print as function, integer division) are now uncontroversially better. But the str/bytes split specifically had to be partially reversed (see PEP 414 + PEP 461) — the assumption 'developers can always do the right thing with text vs bytes at boundaries' was wrong.

entries.title: Immutable bytes and mutable buffer types

load_bearing_assumptions.text: Removing the u'' string literal prefix in Python 3 is safe because all strings are unicode by default — the prefix is redundant.
load_bearing_assumptions.outcome_evidence: PEP 414 (2012) explicitly reinstated u'' literals to ease porting from Python 2. The reasoning in PEP 414's Motivation section is the direct refutation: 'lack of support for the u prefix produces issues for Python 2 compatibility'. The original assumption that u'' could simply go away ignored the cost of writing single-source 2/3 compatible code.

load_bearing_assumptions.text: The print-statement to print() conversion is mechanical and 2to3 will handle it cleanly.
load_bearing_assumptions.outcome_evidence: The 2to3 fixer for print is one of the most reliable transformations; community feedback confirms this is one of the cleaner changes in P3K. Counterexample asymmetry (this one DID work) is part of why a control case matters in the pilot.

load_bearing_assumptions.text: Non-ASCII identifiers will not cause meaningful security or readability problems in practice — code-review and IDEs catch confusable characters.
load_bearing_assumptions.outcome_evidence: Confusable-identifier attacks (homoglyph attacks like Cyrillic а vs Latin a) became a real supply-chain concern in 2017+ and PyPI has had to add detection. The original assumption that 'tooling will catch this' was optimistic — most projects don't lint for it. Not catastrophic, but worse than predicted.

load_bearing_assumptions.text: Bytes don't need %-formatting or .format() — text-formatting belongs on str only.
load_bearing_assumptions.outcome_evidence: PEP 461 (2014) reinstated %-formatting on bytes specifically because real-world wire-protocol code (HTTP, FTP, Email) needs to interpolate into byte strings without a str round-trip. PEP 461's Rationale cites direct user pain.
